title: The Heart of DevOps Culture
description:
## What Is DevOps, Really?

A company decides to "do DevOps." One manager buys a popular CI/CD tool and declares the transformation complete. A year later, dev and ops still blame each other for every outage and deployments are still slow.

What is the **central idea** of DevOps *culture* that this company missed?

options.text: DevOps is a specific tool or platform you install; once it's running, you have DevOps.
DevOps breaks down the silos between development and operations, creating shared ownership and responsibility for the entire software lifecycle.
DevOps is solely the operations team's job — developers just hand off code and move on.
DevOps mainly means writing more documentation so handoffs between teams go smoothly.

explanation: DevOps is fundamentally a **cultural shift**: dev and ops (and often QA and security) stop working as isolated silos and instead **share ownership and responsibility** for code from commit all the way through to running it in production. That shared accountability — "you build it, you run it" — is what actually reduces friction and speeds delivery. Tools (option a) *support* DevOps but cannot create the culture by themselves. Making it only ops's job (c) recreates the very wall DevOps removes. More documentation (d) can help communication but is a tactic, not the core idea — it doesn't change who is accountable for the whole lifecycle.

hints.title: Think about ownership, not software
hints.body: Ask yourself: after adopting DevOps, who is responsible when the app breaks in production at 2am? The answer reveals what changed culturally.
